meta.title: JME — post-withdrawal relapse risk
meta.authors: Stevelink R, Al-Toma D, Jansen FE, Lamberink HJ, Asadi-Pooya AA, Farazdaghi M, et al., Otte WM, Braun KPJ
meta.outcome: Individualised risk of seizure recurrence at 2 and 5 years after withdrawal of antiseizure medication in a currently seizure-free person with juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (JME).
meta.cff_info:
What this shows. For a person with juvenile myoclonic epilepsy who is currently seizure-free and considering stopping antiseizure medication, this estimates the probability of seizure recurrence within 2 and 5 years, from age at withdrawal, how long they have been seizure-free, and how many ASMs they take.

Evidence. Developed in a systematic review and individual participant data meta-analysis. This is the withdrawal companion to the JME drug-resistance model also hosted here.

Note. A longer seizure-free interval lowers the risk; more ASMs and older age at withdrawal raise it. Population-level estimates — always apply clinical judgement, and weigh them against the consequences of a recurrence (e.g. driving).

Source & acknowledgement. This calculator reproduces the Epilepsy Prediction Tools JME medication-withdrawal model from UMC Utrecht and partners. The published look-up table (20,400 rows per horizon) was taken from the authors' reference implementation (github.com/wmotte/epilepsypredictiontools, Apache-2.0) and re-expressed exactly as the underlying proportional-hazards model. Please cite the original paper and visit epilepsypredictiontools.info for the authors' original tool.

source_link.label: original tool
source_link.title: The authors' original tool at epilepsypredictiontools.info (UMC Utrecht)

predictors.name: Age at withdrawal
predictors.assess: Age (years) at which ASM withdrawal is started. Recurrence risk rises with older age at withdrawal.

predictors.name: Seizure-free interval
predictors.assess: Years the person has been seizure-free at the point of withdrawal. A longer seizure-free interval substantially lowers the recurrence risk, with the biggest gains in the first few years.

predictors.name: Number of ASMs at withdrawal
predictors.assess: How many antiseizure medications are being taken at the time of reduction. Each additional drug multiplies the hazard of recurrence.

model.panel_title: Seizure recurrence after ASM withdrawal in JME
model.note: Population-level estimates for JME. Always apply clinical judgement.
model.viz_info: Each pie is the predicted probability of a seizure recurrence by that horizon; the grey remainder is the chance of remaining seizure-free. Below, each factor's contribution to the linear predictor is broken down.

provenance.source: Stevelink et al., eClinicalMedicine 2022;53:101732 (doi 10.1016/j.eclinm.2022.101732, PMID 36467455). The authors' published 20,400-row look-up table (docs/js/jme-withdrawal-calc.js, Apache-2.0, from JME_calculator/jme_one_script/lookup.csv) was taken verbatim from github.com/wmotte/epilepsypredictiontools and inverted to recover the underlying model.
provenance.notes: The published table is a materialisation of a proportional-hazards model: on the complementary log-log scale the effects of age, seizure-free interval and ASM count are additive and separable, and the 5-year table is the 2-year table shifted by a constant K=+0.35940 in every subgroup. Recovered as splines on age (100 knots) and seizure-free interval (51 knots) plus a linear ASM term (+0.36649 per drug), with the two horizons as F0 baselines. Reproduces all 20,400 published cells to <0.05 pp on both horizons — far below the 0.1 pp the authors' own tool displays.
